title: The heart
paperName: Paper 1 Multiple Choice & Paper 2 AS Level Structured Questions
summary: This lesson details the structure and function of the mammalian heart, a key component of the double circulatory system. We will explore the path of blood through its four chambers, the roles of the valves, and the sequence of events in the cardiac cycle (systole and diastole).
learningObjectives: Describe the external and internal structure of the mammalian heart.
Explain the roles of the atria, ventricles, valves, and major blood vessels in the cardiac cycle.
Outline the cardiac cycle, including atrial systole, ventricular systole, and diastole.
Relate the structure of the heart chambers and vessels to their functions, including the differences in wall thickness.

simpleExplanation.title: The Heart's Double Pump
simpleExplanation.summary: The heart acts as two pumps working in sync. One pump sends blood to the lungs to get oxygen, and the other, more powerful pump sends this oxygenated blood to the rest of the body.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine a delivery depot with two loading bays. Bay 1 (the right side) receives deoxygenated parcels (blood) from the body and sends them to the 're-stocking centre' (the lungs). Bay 2 (the left side) receives the re-stocked, oxygenated parcels and sends them out for delivery to the entire town (the body). One-way doors (valves) ensure parcels only move in the correct direction.
steps: Deoxygenated blood from the body enters the right atrium via the vena cava.
The right ventricle pumps this blood to the lungs via the pulmonary artery.
Oxygenated blood returns from the lungs to the left atrium via the pulmonary vein.
The powerful left ventricle pumps this oxygenated blood to the whole body via the aorta.

flashcards.back: Valves located between the atria and ventricles. The tricuspid valve is on the right side, and the bicuspid (mitral) valve is on the left.
Valves located between the ventricles and the major arteries. The pulmonary valve is at the exit of the right ventricle, and the aortic valve is at the exit of the left ventricle.
The phase of the cardiac cycle when the heart muscle contracts, pumping blood out of the chambers. Atrial systole precedes ventricular systole.
The phase of the cardiac cycle when the heart muscle relaxes, allowing the chambers to fill with blood.
Muscle that can contract and relax without receiving signals from the nervous system. Cardiac muscle is myogenic, with its rhythm initiated by the sinoatrial node (SAN).
The muscular wall that separates the left and right sides of the heart, preventing the mixing of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood.
The blood vessels that branch off the aorta to supply the heart muscle itself with oxygen and nutrients. A blockage here causes a heart attack (myocardial infarction).

flashcards.front: Why is the left ventricle wall thicker than the right?
flashcards.back: The left ventricle pumps blood to the entire body (systemic circulation) at high pressure, whereas the right ventricle only pumps blood to the lungs (pulmonary circulation) at a lower pressure.

flashcards.front: Pulmonary Artery vs. Pulmonary Vein: The Exception
flashcards.back: Trap: Arteries usually carry oxygenated blood, but the pulmonary artery carries deoxygenated blood from the heart to the lungs. Veins usually carry deoxygenated blood, but the pulmonary vein carries oxygenated blood from the lungs to the heart.
The volume of blood pumped by one ventricle of the heart in one minute. It is calculated as: Cardiac Output = Heart Rate × Stroke Volume.
The volume of blood pumped out of one ventricle of the heart during one contraction (one heartbeat).
Remember 'TRi' for Right: the TRicuspid valve is in the Right side of the heart. The bicuspid (mitral) valve is therefore in the left.

faq.q: What causes the 'lub-dub' sound of a heartbeat?
faq.a: The sounds are caused by the closing of the heart valves. The first sound, 'lub', is produced by the closing of the atrioventricular (tricuspid and bicuspid) valves at the start of ventricular systole. The second, sharper sound, 'dub', is produced by the closing of the semilunar (aortic and pulmonary) valves at the end of ventricular systole.

faq.q: Why is a double circulatory system more efficient than a single one?
faq.a: In a double circulatory system, blood is pumped to the lungs to be oxygenated and then returns to the heart to be pumped to the rest of the body at high pressure. This ensures that tissues receive blood that is both fully oxygenated and flowing rapidly, allowing for a higher metabolic rate, which is essential for active, warm-blooded animals like mammals.

faq.q: What is the difference between the vena cava and the aorta?
faq.a: The vena cava is the largest vein in the body; it brings deoxygenated blood from the body back to the right atrium of the heart. The aorta is the largest artery; it carries oxygenated blood at high pressure from the left ventricle to the rest of the body.

sections.content: The heart is a muscular organ that acts as the engine of the circulatory system. In mammals, it facilitates a double circulatory system, a highly efficient mechanism that ensures tissues receive a constant supply of oxygenated blood. This lesson will dissect the heart's intricate structure and the precisely coordinated events of the cardiac cycle that make this possible.
Gross Structure of the Mammalian Heart

The heart is a four-chambered organ. The two upper chambers are the atria (singular: atrium), which receive blood returning to the heart. The two lower, more muscular chambers are the ventricles, which pump blood out of the heart. A muscular wall called the septum completely separates the right side from the left side, preventing the mixing of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood. The wall of the left ventricle is significantly thicker and more muscular than the right ventricle, a key structural adaptation related to its function.

items: Four chambers: right atrium, right ventricle, left atrium, left ventricle.
Valves ensure unidirectional blood flow: atrioventricular (tricuspid, bicuspid) and semilunar (aortic, pulmonary).
Septum prevents mixing of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood.
Major vessels: Vena Cava & Pulmonary Artery (right side), Pulmonary Vein & Aorta (left side).

sections.content: The Path of Blood: A Double Circuit

Blood follows a specific pathway through the heart in two distinct circuits. The pulmonary circuit involves the right side of the heart. Deoxygenated blood enters the right atrium from the body via the vena cava, passes through the tricuspid valve into the right ventricle, and is then pumped past the pulmonary valve into the pulmonary artery, which leads to the lungs. The systemic circuit involves the left side. Oxygenated blood returns from the lungs via the pulmonary vein into the left atrium, passes through the bicuspid (mitral) valve into the left ventricle, and is then forcefully pumped past the aortic valve into the aorta for distribution to the rest of the body.

The cardiac cycle describes the sequence of events in a single heartbeat, typically lasting about 0.8 seconds at rest. It consists of three main phases: 
1. **Diastole:** The entire heart is relaxed. Blood flows passively from the atria into the ventricles. The atrioventricular valves are open, and the semilunar valves are closed. 
2. **Atrial Systole:** The atria contract, forcing the remaining blood into the ventricles. 
3. **Ventricular Systole:** The ventricles contract. The pressure increase closes the atrioventricular valves (producing the 'lub' sound) and opens the semilunar valves, ejecting blood into the aorta and pulmonary artery. As the ventricles relax, pressure drops, and the back pressure of blood in the arteries closes the semilunar valves (producing the 'dub' sound).

sections.question: An athlete has a resting heart rate of 50 beats per minute and a stroke volume of 140 cm³ per beat. Calculate their cardiac output in dm³ per minute.
sections.solution:
Step 1: State the formula for cardiac output.
Cardiac Output = Heart Rate × Stroke Volume

Step 2: Substitute the given values into the formula.
Cardiac Output = 50 beats min⁻¹ × 140 cm³ beat⁻¹
Cardiac Output = 7000 cm³ min⁻¹

Step 3: Convert the units from cm³ to dm³ as required by the question. (Remember: 1000 cm³ = 1 dm³)
Cardiac Output = 7000 / 1000 dm³ min⁻¹
Cardiac Output = 7.0 dm³ min⁻¹

[1 mark for formula/correct calculation, 1 mark for correct unit conversion and final answer]

sections.content: When labelling diagrams of the heart, remember that you are viewing it from the front. This means the left ventricle is on the right side of the page, and the right ventricle is on the left. Always double-check the labels for 'left' and 'right' to avoid simple mistakes.

sections.question: Explain why the wall of the left ventricle is significantly thicker than the wall of the right ventricle.
sections.solution:
1. The left ventricle pumps blood into the aorta to be distributed to the entire body (systemic circulation). [1 mark]
2. This requires generating a much higher pressure to overcome the greater resistance of the extensive systemic capillary network. [1 mark]
3. The right ventricle only pumps blood to the lungs (pulmonary circulation), which is a much shorter distance with lower resistance. [1 mark]
4. Therefore, the thicker, more muscular wall of the left ventricle allows it to contract more forcefully to generate the necessary high pressure for systemic circulation. [1 mark]

sections.content: The heart muscle (myocardium) is too thick to receive sufficient oxygen and nutrients by diffusion from the blood passing through its chambers. It has its own dedicated blood supply via the coronary arteries. These arteries are the very first branches off the aorta, ensuring the hard-working cardiac muscle receives freshly oxygenated blood at high pressure. If a coronary artery becomes blocked, for example by an atherosclerotic plaque, the region of heart muscle it supplies is deprived of oxygen, leading to a myocardial infarction, or heart attack.

sections.label: Test your knowledge on the heart
